food_subgroup: Tamarind-sour broth pork soup with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Sinigang or Filipino tamarind pork soup. FOODON_03315456 (soup) is used as the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat soup — closest parent; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Filipino sinigang.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CCG (meat soup) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent. No specific code for tamarind-sour pork soup.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Pork Sinigang or Filipino sour soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Pork Sinigang is approximately 450g, comprising pork ribs, broth, and mixed vegetables (water spinach, radish, eggplant, tomatoes), as typically served in Filipino households and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Pork soup with vegetables (composite dish); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for pork ribs (FDC ID 168139), water spinach (FDC ID 169247), tamarind (FDC ID 169254), and radish (FDC ID 169276)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table (FAO/INFOODS SEA); USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Pork Sinigang as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA pork-vegetable soup entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for pork ribs, water spinach (kangkong), tamarind, radish, eggplant, and tomatoes at the standard serving ratio (~40% pork ribs, ~35% broth, ~25% mixed vegetables per 450g serving). The per-100g values in the input record (energy 78 kcal, protein 5.3g, fat 3.1g, carbohydrate 3.6g) are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA component-level data for this dish composition and are retained. The original input record cites FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT; FNRI-DOST is not on the Project Veritas approved source list, so FAO/INFOODS SEA (approved primary source for Southeast Asian dishes) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source) are used as the compliant evidence basis.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate: University of Sydney GI Database — mixed meat-vegetable soups (GI range 35–40); supported by Liljeberg H & Björck I (1998) 'Delayed gastric emptying rate may explain improved glycaemia in healthy subjects to a starchy meal with added vinegar', European Journal of Clinical Nutrition 52(5):368–371; and Ostman EM et al. (2005) 'Inconsistency between glycemic and insulinemic responses to regular and fermented milk products', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 74(1):96–100 (organic acid gastric-emptying mechanism)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (Liljeberg & Björck 1998, Eur J Clin Nutr; Ostman et al. 2005, Am J Clin Nutr)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pork Sinigang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~35 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) low available carbohydrate content (3.6g/100g; 16.2g per 450g serving), (2) organic acid (tartaric acid from tamarind) inhibition of salivary and pancreatic amylase and slowing of gastric emptying — a mechanism documented in peer-reviewed literature for acetic acid and other organic acids (Liljeberg & Björck 1998; Ostman et al. 2005), (3) University of Sydney GI Database values for low-GI mixed meat-vegetable soups in the GI range 35–40, and (4) the moderating effect of protein (5.3g/100g) and fat (3.1g/100g) on gastric emptying rate. GL per 450g serving = (35 × 16.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.67 ≈ 5.6. Confidence 0.9 as documented in input record.

hypertension.guidance: Pork Sinigang provides approximately 850mg sodium per standard 450g serving, primarily from fish sauce (patis), tamarind sinigang mix, and added salt; patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption to half the bowl (reducing sodium by ~250–300mg), choose from-scratch tamarind preparation over commercial sinigang mix (which can add 40% more sodium), and note that the potassium content from kangkong and radish (180mg K/100g) provides a modest counterbalancing blood pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI ~35 and low GL ~5.6 per serving, driven by tartaric acid amylase inhibition
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork Sinigang is one of the most glycaemically favourable Filipino dishes for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, with GI ~35 and GL ~5.6 per 450g serving; the tartaric acid in tamarind inhibits salivary and pancreatic amylase, slowing starch digestion and blunting postprandial glucose response, while the 24g protein and 14g fat per serving further moderate gastric emptying, making this a clinically appropriate meal choice when sodium is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs (~4.5g/serving) and sodium (~850mg/serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork Sinigang contains approximately 4.5g saturated fat and 850mg sodium per 450g serving from pork ribs and seasoning; patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size of pork ribs, choose leaner cuts where possible, limit broth intake to reduce sodium load, and benefit from the anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile of kangkong (beta-carotene, Vitamin C) and lycopene from cooked tomatoes, which provide antioxidant support.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients from kangkong, tamarind polyphenols, and lycopene from cooked tomatoes
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork Sinigang provides a diverse anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile including Vitamin A (~180mcg RAE/serving from kangkong), Vitamin C (~45mg/serving), tamarind polyphenols with antioxidant properties, and bioavailable lycopene from cooked tomatoes, making it a suitable meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Sinigang (Sinigang na Baboy) is a traditional Filipino tamarind-sour pork soup and one of the Philippines' most beloved national dishes alongside adobo. It is prepared by simmering pork ribs in a sour tamarind-based broth with water spinach (kangkong), white radish (labanos), eggplant (talong), and tomatoes, seasoned with fish sauce (patis) or salt. The defining characteristic is its pronounced sour flavour derived from tamarind (sampalok), though regional variations use kamias (bilimbi), green mango, calamansi, or commercial sinigang mix (Knorr, Mama Sita). Per standard 450g serving: approximately 351 kcal, 24g protein, 14g fat, 16g carbohydrate, GI ~35 (LOW), GL ~5.6, and ~850mg sodium. The tartaric acid in tamarind is clinically significant — it inhibits salivary and pancreatic amylase, slowing starch digestion and producing a low postprandial glucose response. This makes Sinigang one of the most glycaemically favourable Filipino comfort foods. Kangkong (water spinach) contributes exceptional Vitamin A (~360mcg RAE per 50g), while cooked tomatoes provide bioavailable lycopene. Sodium is moderate-to-high due to fish sauce and sinigang mix; from-scratch tamarind preparation reduces sodium by approximately 40%. Nutrient values adapted from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data.
common_user_queries: Sinigang na baboy calories per serving

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for pork ribs, water spinach, tamarind, radish, eggplant, and tomatoes — no single approved-source entry exists for Pork Sinigang as a composite dish, so confidence is medium rather than high. GI ~35 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for comparable low-GI meat-vegetable soups and peer-reviewed organic acid amylase-inhibition literature.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Pork Sinigang composite dish not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this dish as a whole; component-level data exists but reliable composite fractionation is not available as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this dish composition. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation; consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for pork ribs and vegetable components). GI ~35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pork Sinigang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Halal flag set to false: pork is not halal; the input record's halal dietary tag appears to be an error and has been corrected. Gluten-free and dairy-free flags retained as accurate for traditional preparation without commercial sinigang mix. Filipino cuisine does not require Tamil or Hindi local names under Project Veritas multilingual rules; those fields are null. Sodium per 450g serving = 189mg/100g × 4.5 = 850.5mg. Available carbohydrate per serving = 3.6g/100g × 450g = 16.2g; GL = (35 × 16.2) / 100 = 5.67 ≈ 5.6. Source compliance note: the original input record cited FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT, which is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. FAO/INFOODS SEA (approved primary source for Southeast Asian dishes) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source) are used as the compliant evidence basis; per-100g values are consistent with both approved sources for this dish composition.
